caveats: a: The occupation and IREM evidence span owner and third-party managers, residential and nonresidential properties, and firms larger than the frozen lens.
a: Task lists and technology use do not provide wage-weighted time shares.
a: Office, retail, industrial, medical, lodging, and other buildings have different labor mixes and automation potential.
rho: Use of AI in any workflow is not the share of exposed work operationally implemented.
rho: The IREM sample is not specific to LMM 531120 owner-lessors and likely overrepresents professional managers.
rho: Building-system interoperability and data quality vary substantially by asset vintage.
e: No source directly measures eligibility within the supplied LMM band.

e: The frozen firm count uses a 3.96% Real Estate Operations and Services margin associated with agency, brokerage, and property-management economics; normalized owner-lessor earnings and depreciation treatment can differ sharply, so band membership may be materially misstated.
e: One beneficial owner may hold separate buildings in many legal entities.
s5: No eligible-firm denominator or owner-succession cohort was observed.
s5: Asset and portfolio sales are not necessarily operating-company control transfers.
s5: Partnership interest transfers and beneficial-owner changes are often private.
q: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived gross benefit.
q: Gross, modified-gross, and net lease structures allocate expenses differently.
q: Savings produced by outsourced managers may accrue to the manager rather than the owner-lessor.
d5: Property-manager employment is not constant-price demand for 531120 space or operating services.
d5: Work-at-home and e-commerce shares are national behavior indicators, not direct five-year space-demand forecasts.
d5: The CBECS growth comparison ends in 2018 and predates pandemic-era office changes.
d5: The frozen lens's property-type and geographic mix is unknown.
o: Triple-net tenants assume more operating responsibility than tenants under gross leases.
o: Industrial, office, retail, medical, lodging, and mixed-use buildings require different operator involvement.
o: Third-party property managers can substitute for direct owner-lessor staffing without eliminating building demand.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business and institutional tenants pay recurring base rent and, depending on lease structure, reimburse some taxes, insurance, utilities, maintenance, or common-area costs; the lessor provides usable space and remains responsible directly or through vendors for lease administration and building stewardship.
lens.excluded_activities: Third-party property managers, self-storage lessors, passive single-asset shells without a transferable operating organization, captive internal real-estate units, non-control financing vehicles, venue operators classified elsewhere, and firms outside the approximately $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market owner-lessors and master lessors of office, retail, industrial, medical, lodging, and other nonresidential buildings, excluding self-storage, that repeatedly lease space to external tenants and retain accountable leasing, building-operation, maintenance, compliance, or vendor-oversight functions.

a.bridge: BLS tasks for property, real-estate, and community-association managers are mapped to the direct wage bill of nonresidential lessors: lease administration, collections, accounts payable, reports, routine tenant communications, document review, and maintenance triage are treated as substantially AI-exposed, while inspections, contractor supervision, repairs, negotiations, emergency response, and tenant disputes remain human-led. The occupation includes residential and third-party management and supplies no 531120 task-time weights; focusing on managerial workflows likely overstates exposure where direct payroll is maintenance-heavy, while omitting clerical and accounting occupations pushes the other way.
caveats: The occupation and IREM evidence span owner and third-party managers, residential and nonresidential properties, and firms larger than the frozen lens.
Task lists and technology use do not provide wage-weighted time shares.
Office, retail, industrial, medical, lodging, and other buildings have different labor mixes and automation potential.
a.change_evidence: A 531120 payroll-weighted task study segmented by property type and role, covering lease administration, accounting, tenant service, facilities engineering, maintenance, security coordination, and asset management, net of current automation.
a.rationale: Commercial-property operations contain document-heavy leases, recurring invoices, tenant requests, budgets, energy and building data, and vendor workflows that are amenable to AI support. Physical building stewardship, complex negotiations, life-safety decisions, and tenant-specific exceptions constrain substitution.

d5.bridge: BLS projects property-manager employment to grow 4% from 2024 to 2034, which implies about 1.02 over five years when square-rooted, but that occupation mixes residential and third-party management. The base is adjusted below that proxy because 35% of employed people worked at home on days worked in 2025 and e-commerce reached 16.9% of first-quarter 2026 retail sales while growing faster than total retail sales, pressuring portions of office and retail demand. EIA's older CBECS shows commercial buildings and floorspace grew 6% and 11% from 2012 to 2018, supporting an offsetting durable physical-stock base. Remote-work and e-commerce indicators may overstate losses for industrial, medical, lodging, and specialized buildings, while the old aggregate stock trend may overstate current office and retail durability.
caveats: Property-manager employment is not constant-price demand for 531120 space or operating services.
Work-at-home and e-commerce shares are national behavior indicators, not direct five-year space-demand forecasts.
The CBECS growth comparison ends in 2018 and predates pandemic-era office changes.
The frozen lens's property-type and geographic mix is unknown.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted occupied-square-foot and same-property rent cohorts by office, retail, industrial, medical, lodging, and mixed-use property, with vacancy, demolitions, conversions, remote-work exposure, and quality adjustments over five years.
d5.rationale: Nonresidential demand is a weighted mix of challenged office and some retail space, comparatively durable medical and service buildings, and logistics or specialized space that may benefit from e-commerce and economic growth. A modest aggregate decline is plausible, but the wide range reflects unknown property mix and local market conditions.

caveats: No source directly measures eligibility within the supplied LMM band.

The frozen firm count uses a 3.96% Real Estate Operations and Services margin associated with agency, brokerage, and property-management economics; normalized owner-lessor earnings and depreciation treatment can differ sharply, so band membership may be materially misstated.
One beneficial owner may hold separate buildings in many legal entities.
e.change_evidence: A stratified beneficial-owner review of 531120 employer firms using commercial databases, state entities, CoStar, and property records, coding employee functions, property count and type, related-party leases, third-party management, normalized NOI, and separability of the operating platform from the real estate.
e.rationale: Census includes owner-lessors and sublessors that may self-manage or outsource property management. Many nominal firms are passive, single-asset, tax, or financing structures rather than transferable operating platforms, while multi-property lessors with internal leasing, facilities, tenant-service, and vendor functions remain eligible.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 531120 ($16,753,099,110) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 531120 ($144,482,064,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0396 (margins.json[53]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Real Estate (Operations & Services) EBITDA/Sales 3.96% (agency/brokerage/property-management economics, dominant in firm counts)); revenue/employee $837,815 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 531120); class <5: 24,619 firms, avg revenue/firm $760,623 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,121 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,370 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,521,470 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $99,850 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,520 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,270,264 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $248,302 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,168 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,321,961 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $844,350 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 349 firms, avg revenue/firm $101,283,330 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,010,820 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 234 firms, avg revenue/firm $202,889,692 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,034,432 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 583

caveats: Triple-net tenants assume more operating responsibility than tenants under gross leases.
Industrial, office, retail, medical, lodging, and mixed-use buildings require different operator involvement.
Third-party property managers can substitute for direct owner-lessor staffing without eliminating building demand.
o.change_evidence: Building-level studies of remote operations, automated lease administration, BMS coverage, tenant self-service resolution, onsite staffing, safety and compliance incidents, emergency calls, and outsourced management by property type.
o.rationale: Lease documents, payments, tenant requests, access, and some building controls can become self-service or software-mediated. However, occupied commercial buildings still require accountable parties for lease exceptions, life safety, physical access, inspections, maintenance, contractors, regulatory compliance, capital decisions, and tenant disputes; the requirement varies by lease and building type.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived gross benefit.
Gross, modified-gross, and net lease structures allocate expenses differently.
Savings produced by outsourced managers may accrue to the manager rather than the owner-lessor.
q.change_evidence: Lease-level and property P&L cohorts before and after automation, including base rent, expense recoveries, free-rent and tenant-improvement concessions, payroll, property-management fees, software, integration cost, renewal spreads, and vacancy by building type.
q.rationale: Multi-year commercial leases make rent largely independent of administrative labor hours, and some operating expenses are contractually reimbursed, so implemented productivity can be retained for meaningful periods. Retention is reduced by renewal competition, tenant concessions, expense-recovery mechanics, service-level commitments, software and integration costs, and capital spending needed to support digital operations.

rho.bridge: IREM's 2025 Proptech Insights reports 46.3% of respondents using AI across accounts payable, collections, lease abstraction, and building-management-system operations, while 44.2% cite total cost, 38.8% integration, and 37.9% cost-benefit determination as implementation hurdles. These current use and constraint shares are translated into the five-year implemented share of the defined exposed opportunity. IREM respondents are professional real-estate managers with unusually high technology engagement, likely biasing LMM owner-lessor implementation upward; continuing embedded software adoption over five years offsets part of that bias.
caveats: Use of AI in any workflow is not the share of exposed work operationally implemented.
The IREM sample is not specific to LMM 531120 owner-lessors and likely overrepresents professional managers.
Building-system interoperability and data quality vary substantially by asset vintage.
rho.change_evidence: Longitudinal IREM or BOMA deployment data segmented by owner-lessor size and building type, with measured utilization, workflow completion, exception rates, labor hours, integration cost, and building-management-system coverage.
rho.rationale: AI and proptech are already present in core commercial-property workflows, supporting implementation beyond pilots. Legacy building systems, fragmented lease data, vendor integrations, cybersecurity, capital cost, reliability, and the need for human approval limit straight-through adoption.

caveats: No eligible-firm denominator or owner-succession cohort was observed.
Asset and portfolio sales are not necessarily operating-company control transfers.
Partnership interest transfers and beneficial-owner changes are often private.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of MSCI Real Capital Analytics, CoStar, PitchBook, S&P Capital IQ, deed, and beneficial-ownership records matched to eligible firms, separating platform control sales from property sales, recapitalizations, fund transfers, and internal restructurings.
s5.rationale: Commercial buildings and portfolios transact, but those are usually asset sales rather than qualifying transfers of an operating lessor. Control transfers of eligible multi-property platforms occur, yet single-property entities, joint ventures, recapitalizations, and internal reorganizations make visible real-estate deal flow a poor firm-transition denominator.

principal_driver: Long-duration lease revenue can retain workflow savings while complex occupied buildings continue to require accountable physical and contractual stewardship.
principal_weakness: The broad code mixes sharply different property-demand trajectories and contains many asset-holding entities that are not transferable operating-service platforms.

sources.supports: Defines 531120 as owner-lessors and sublessors of nonresidential buildings other than self-storage and states that establishments may self-manage or use another establishment to manage property.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau NAICS Sector 53: 531120 Lessors of Nonresidential Buildings

sources.supports: Lists leasing, collections, budgeting, records, inspections, repairs, contracting, complaint, and compliance duties; notes some task automation; and projects 4% occupation employment growth from 2024 to 2034.
sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Property, Real Estate, and Community Association Managers

sources.supports: Reports 46.3% of respondents using AI in workflows including accounts payable, collections, lease abstraction, and BMS operations, and identifies cost, integration, and cost-benefit hurdles.
sources.title: IREM Proptech Insights 2025

sources.supports: Reports e-commerce at 16.9% of seasonally adjusted retail sales in first-quarter 2026, with year-over-year e-commerce growth of 9.8% versus 3.9% for total retail sales.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau Quarterly Retail E-Commerce Sales, First Quarter 2026

sources.supports: Reports that 35% of employed people did some or all work at home on days they worked in 2025, including 51% of workers with a bachelor's degree or higher.
sources.title: BLS American Time Use Survey: 2025 Results

sources.supports: Reports 5.9 million commercial buildings and 97 billion square feet in 2018, with building count up 6% and floorspace up 11% from 2012, while identifying variation by building activity.
sources.title: U.S. EIA: Commercial Buildings Have Gotten Larger in the United States

title: Lessors of Nonresidential Buildings (except Miniwarehouses)
